channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘The Devil Wears Prada’ Then and Now: Why It Hits Differently
item.description: The film’s portrait of the work lives of a young journalist and a top editor holds lessons for us. It’s just that the lessons have changed with time.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘Fill the Stage!’ Tiler Peck Says It’s Time for Ballet to Go Big
item.description: For her second commission at New York City Ballet, the principal dancer is leveling up. “Symphonie Espagnole,” with a nod to the company’s Balanchine heritage, has a cast of 40.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas and Thea Traff
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times

item.title: TV Shows Are Changing How Menopause Is Portrayed Onscreen
item.description: Sitcoms have long treated menopause as a punchline, but newer series like “Your Friends & Neighbors” and “Riot Women” depict it as a matter-of-fact part of women’s lives.
item.dc:creator: Clarissa Cruz

item.title: In ‘Rocky Horror,’ Luke Evans Finds His Ballad of Sexual Liberation
item.description: What does it take to play Frank-N-Furter in “The Rocky Horror Show” on Broadway? Luke Evans transforms in five-inch heels and an endless supply of glitter.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small and Justin J Wee
item.media:credit: Justin J Wee for The New York Times

item.title: Revenge, Served Two Ways: Cold in ‘Othello’ and Lukewarm in ‘Hamlet’
item.description: Shakespeare’s brooding prince comes off as bored at the Brooklyn Academy of Music. But Bedlam’s lean production of “Othello” is positively thrilling.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: Khaled Sabsabi’s Rocky Road From Australia to the Biennale
item.description: The Lebanese-Australian artist almost lost his Venice Biennale commission after he was accused of supporting terrorism. Reinstated, he is arriving in Venice showing not one but two works.
item.dc:creator: Will Higginbotham
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times
item.media:description: Khaled Sabsabi at his exhibition at the Australia Pavilion of the Venice Biennale, in April.

item.title: ‘The Other Bennet Sister’ Celebrates Jane Austen’s Awkward, Lovable Middle Sister
item.description: Revisiting “Pride and Prejudice” from Mary Bennet’s point of view, “The Other Bennet Sister” from BritBox offers a different type of onscreen heroine.
item.dc:creator: Eleanor Stanford
item.media:credit: James Pardon/Bad Wolf/BBC
item.media:description: Ella Bruccoleri as Mary Bennet in “The Other Bennet Sister,” the BBC’s most-watched new drama of the past year in Britain.

item.title: ‘Serial Mom’: The Mother’s Day Movie for Our Times
item.description: In this parody of the true crime genre, a mother with murderous tendencies reflects on the hard work and thankless sacrifices that go into parenting.
item.dc:creator: Dina Gachman

item.title: Towa Bird Can Shred With the Pop Stars and the Riot Grrrls
item.description: The 27-year-old singer, songwriter and guitarist got her start on TikTok in 2020. Now she’s releasing “Gentleman,” her second LP of confident, clever rock songs.
item.dc:creator: Phoebe Reilly and Adali Schell
item.media:description: “Gentleman,” the follow-up to Towa Bird’s 2024 debut, “American Hero,” is due May 15.

item.title: When ‘The Late Show With Stephen Colbert’ Goes Away, What Do We Lose?
item.description: Our attachment to an institution may seem counterintuitive, especially with comedy, a rebellious art form. But with Stephen Colbert’s program, there was a lot at stake.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Celina Pereira; Photographs, via Getty Images

item.title: David Letterman Reflects on ‘The Late Show’ and Has Harsh Words for New CBS Owners
item.description: He no longer feels a sense of ownership, but the program’s former host has harsh words for the network.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Sarah Silbiger for The New York Times
item.media:description: David Letterman likened what’s happened with his former show to “driving by your old neighborhood and realizing that where you used to live, they’re putting up an adult bookstore.”

item.title: New Musicals ‘Schmigadoon!’ and ‘Lost Boys’ Lead List of Tony Nominees
item.description: The nominated performers include the film stars Daniel Radcliffe, Rose Byrne, John Lithgow and Lesley Manville.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: “Schmigadoon!” is adapted from a comedic Apple TV series and received 12 Tony nominations, tied with “The Lost Boys” for the most.

item.title: Tony Nominations Snubs and Surprises: Lea Michele Left Out, ‘Titaníque’ Shines
item.description: Adrien Brody, Jean Smart and other boldface names were left out, while June Squibb, André De Shields and Layton Williams as an iceberg were among the surprises.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw, Jesse Green, Alexis Soloski, Scott Heller, Elisabeth Vincentelli and Laura Collins-Hughes
item.media:credit: Photographs by Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Clockwise from top left, Lea Michele and Nicholas Christopher in “Chess”; Adrien Brody in “The Fear of 13”; Rachel Dratch in “The Rocky Horror Show”; and André De Shields in “Cats: The Jellicle Ball.”

item.title: Tony Awards Nominations 2026: The Complete List
item.description: Nominations for the 79th Tony Awards were announced on Tuesday. Here’s who made the list.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.category: Two Strangers (Carry a Cake Across New York) (Play)
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: A scene from “The Lost Boys,” which earned 12 Tony nominations including best new musical.

item.title: Late Night Isn’t Buying Trump’s New ‘Skirmish’ in Iran
item.description: After Trump used the terms “miniwar” and “excursion” to describe the conflict in Iran, Jimmy Kimmel predicted that the president would next call the war “a tiff. It will be a $200 billion tiff.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: ‘The Book of Mormon’ Will Close for 2 Weeks After Fire
item.description: The long-running musical, one of Broadway’s biggest hits, will be closed through at least May 17 after an electrical fire in its lighting booth.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: New York City Department of Buildings
item.media:description: Fire damage at the Eugene O’Neill Theater, which has housed “The Book of Mormon” for 15 years.

item.title: George Herms Dies at 90; Turned Castoff Objects Into Art
item.description: One of the last Beat Generation artists, he embraced the messiness of everyday life, making collages and assemblages from rusty junk and decaying objects.
item.dc:creator: Jori Finkel
item.media:credit: Ryan Miller/WireImage, via Getty Images
item.media:description: George Herms in 2011 with one of his assemblages. “George could make such elegance of mess,” one curator said.

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Some Delight to Your Day
item.description: Buried treasure, birds on the move and secret library access.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Epic Fantasy and Sci-Fi Books for Adults
item.description: The best-selling author Fonda Lee recommends fantasy and science fiction novels with older, wiser, absolutely epic heroes.
item.dc:creator: Fonda Lee
item.media:credit: Author photo by Michael Verity

item.title: Late Night Cringes at Trump’s Chatter in Front of Children
item.description: “How are you talking about nuclear war in front of children?” Desi Lydic asked after the president discussed the Iran conflict in a room full of students on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Kid Cudi Fires M.I.A. From Tour After Onstage Comments About Immigrants
item.description: M.I.A. made the comments at a concert in Dallas, drawing boos from the audience and criticism online.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Rick Kern/Getty Images
item.media:description: The rapper M.I.A. during a concert in Austin, Texas, last week. She was dropped from a tour with Kid Cudi.

item.title: A Loft with Past Lives Gets an Owner Making a New One
item.description: How Jessica Helfand, an artist, started a new chapter in an industrial building.
item.dc:creator: Diana Budds and Jane Beiles
item.media:description: Jessica Helfand in her studio in the repurposed loft. The studio benefits from indirect light streaming in throughout the day.

item.title: Donlyn Lyndon, Last Surviving Creator of the Sea Ranch, Dies at 90
item.description: With his classmates Charles Moore, William Turnbull Jr. and Richard Whitaker, he designed an icon of modern architecture on a windswept bluff in Northern California.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green

item.title: María Nieves Rego, Who Helped Spark a Tango Renaissance, Dies at 91
item.description: With her dance partner and onetime husband, Juan Carlos Copes, she reinvented tango for a global stage, including in the hit stage show “Tango Argentino.”
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: 6 Stellar All-Female Country Duets
item.description: Hear Kacey Musgraves and Miranda Lambert making peace on the record, and more outstanding collaborations between women in Nashville.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: From left: Frank Micelotta/The Walt Disney Company; Christopher Polk/Billboard
item.media:description: Miranda Lambert, left, and Kacey Musgraves worked out their differences on “Horses and Divorces.”

item.title: For Michael Tilson Thomas’s Legacy, Look at Young Musicians
item.description: Thomas, the beloved mentor and conductor of the San Francisco Symphony, recently died. He offered a road map for a 21st-century career in music.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Edgar
item.media:credit: Lauren Lancaster for The New York Times
item.media:description: The conductor Michael Tilson Thomas, who died on April 22 at 81.

item.title: Was It Art? Was It Fashion? Was It Good?
item.description: Guests at the Met Gala had different interpretations of the night’s dress code.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kim Kardashian, in an orange fiberglass breastplate by the British pop artist Allen Jones.

item.title: With Free Vodka, and a Few Protests, Russia Returns to the Venice Biennale
item.description: On Tuesday, Russia opened a pavilion at the Venice Biennale for the first since its full-scale invasion of Ukraine. Ukraine’s own exhibit is just a stone’s throw.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times

item.title: He Left the Berlin Philharmonic to Find His Voice. He Found a World Stage.
item.description: The viola player Brett Dean took a risk that paid off when he left the orchestra to become a full-time composer.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Arlo Brown
item.media:credit: Sandra Singh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brett Dean is a rare case: the orchestra member turned composer. His music, of impeccable craft and unusual verve, is performed frequently by the world’s leading orchestras.

item.title: Inside the Met Gala After-Parties: See the Stars and Their Looks
item.description: Models, designers, D.J.s and performers kept the night going into the early morning.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Bernstein and Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Rebecca Smeyne for The New York Times

item.title: Venice Biennale: In Photos and Video
item.description: Scenes from the highly anticipated art event.
item.dc:creator: Matteo de Mayda

item.title: Five Publishers and Scott Turow Sue Meta and Mark Zuckerberg
item.description: The class-action lawsuit accuses the tech giant and its founder and chief executive of infringing on authors’ copyrights.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Mark Zuckerberg in March 2026.

item.title: The Venice Biennale and Its Many Flashpoints: An Explainer
item.description: The sudden death of a curator, the surprise participation of Russia and an unconventional U.S. entry are heating up the art world Olympics, which begins previews Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: The central pavilion of the Giardini on Tuesday, first day of previews for the 61st Venice Biennale. The exhibition, “In Minor Keys,” was initiated by Koyo Kouoh and overseen by her team after her death last year.

item.title: Why Lauren Halsey Has Collected More Than 2,000 Black Figurines
item.description: Lauren Halsey’s porcelain Black statuettes, collected over two decades, depict people singing in church, playing in the park and other scenes of everyday life.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Acheampong

item.title: A Forum for African Voices Draws Inspiration From a Former Champion
item.description: The African Art in Venice Forum is continuing its mission of addressing the continent’s artistic needs, even as it mourns the loss of Koyo Kouoh, a key supporter.
item.dc:creator: Tariro Mzezewa
item.category: African Art in Venice Forum (African Art Dialogues)

item.title: Late Night Laments Losing Spirit Airlines as a Punchline
item.description: The budget carrier announced that “‘all flights have been canceled, and customer service is no longer available,’ which, coincidentally, was also Spirit’s motto,” Stephen Colbert said.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)

item.title: Kennedy Center Ticketing Union Files Labor Charge Over Layoffs
item.description: The International Alliance of Theatrical Stage Employees challenged the terminations in a complaint to the National Labor Relations Board.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: International Alliance of Theatrical Stage Employees
Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Julia Demaree Nikhinson/Associated Press
item.media:description: The union representing ticketing staff is challenging the layoff of its members at the Kennedy Center, which is preparing to shut down for two years, starting in July.

item.title: Gabriela Lena Frank’s ‘Picaflor’ Wins Pulitzer for Music
item.description: Gabriela Lena Frank’s “Picaflor: A Future Myth,” premiered by the Philadelphia Orchestra, has won the prestigious award for music.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:credit: Carlos Chavarria for The New York Times
item.media:description: The composer Gabriela Lena Frank, whose work “Picaflor: A Future Myth” won the Pulitzer Prize for music.

item.title: ‘Liberation,’ a Memory Play About ’70s Feminism, Wins Drama Pulitzer
item.description: The prize board called the playwright Bess Wohl’s work “a striking blend of comedy and sincerity.”
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Jeenah Moon for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bess Wohl was partly inspired by her mother, a Ms. magazine contributor who belonged to a consciousness-raising group.

item.title: Blake Lively and Justin Baldoni Settle ‘It Ends With Us’ Lawsuit
item.description: Ms. Lively had accused Mr. Baldoni of waging a smear campaign against her after she complained of harassment when making a film together. He had denied her accusation.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: Blake Lively and Justin Baldoni starred in the 2024 movie “It Ends With Us.”

item.title: The Books That Won the 2026 Pulitzer Prizes
item.description: “We the People,” by Jill Lepore, won the history prize, and Daniel Kraus received the fiction prize for “Angel Down.”
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter, Joumana Khatib and Gregory Cowles
item.category: We the People: A History of the US Constitution (Book)
Pride and Pleasure: The Schuyler Sisters in an Age of Revolution (Book)
There Is No Place for Us: Working and Homeless in America (Book)
item.media:credit: Left: Kayana Szymczak for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jill Lepore won the 2026 Pulitzer Prize for a book of history for “We the People: A History of the U.S. Constitution”

item.title: Review: Out, Damned Patriarchy! A Revisionist Ballet ‘Macbeth’
item.description: In Akram Khan’s new full-length work for Royal Danish Ballet, Lady Macbeth is a pure-intentioned heroine.
item.dc:creator: Roslyn Sulcas

item.title: Venice Showcases an Array of Global Art Stars During the Biennale
item.description: Marina Abramovic, Lorna Simpson, Arthur Jafa, Richard Prince and Peggy Guggenheim are among those being featured at museums and foundations during this year’s international art exhibition.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri

item.title: A Dutch Art Studio Lights Up Venice’s Grand Canal
item.description: Lonneke Gordijn and Ralph Nauta of Studio Drift have brought their kinetic light sculptures outdoors at the Venice Biennale.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal

item.title: For the Painter Michael Armitage, Art Comes With a ‘Sense of Responsibility’
item.description: The Kenyan British artist Michael Armitage, who is showing his paintings and studies in Venice, reflects on his Kenyan roots and his recent move to Indonesia.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri

item.title: At 90, the Korean artist Lee Ufan Finds Energy in His Art
item.description: The Korean artist Lee Ufan continues to work every day, and has three exhibitions on the horizon, including a display of his pivotal paintings at the Venice Biennale.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Russeth and Matteo de Mayda
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lee has painted two radiant little pieces on a floor and a wall at the San Marco Art Centre in Venice as part of his exhibition there during the Venice Biennale.

item.title: In Venice, an Ocean-Inspired Exhibition Takes Visitors Under the Sea
item.description: Inside a former convent on an island, immersive artworks invite visitors to “stretch their sense of self to include the vastness of the ocean.”
item.dc:creator: Keridwen Cornelius

item.title: Electrical Fire at Broadway Theater Closes ‘Book of Mormon’ Indefinitely
item.description: The New York Fire Department said a room in the Eugene O’Neill Theater had incurred “substantial damage.” Producers haven’t said when performances will resume.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson and Davaughnia Wilson
item.media:credit: Liam Stack/The New York Times
item.media:description: The three-alarm fire prompted a response from nearly 200 firefighters and emergency medical workers. The theater has said it needs time to assess the damage.

item.title: A Show in Rome Sheds New Light on Francesca Woodman’s Haunting Images
item.description: The American photographer died at just 22, and yet the beautiful, unsettling work she created continues to influence art and artists. Why?
item.dc:creator: Dale Berning Sawa

item.title: In Venice, Meditating on Time and Place on a Leisurely Bar Crawl
item.description: “You can’t escape drink in Venice,” one patron noted. “It’s a ritual.” An adopted Venetian, a reporter and a photographer wandered the city’s watering holes over one long aperitivo hour.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times
item.media:description: A view of the San Basegio Canal in Venice. The island city has retained a highly distinctive bar culture.

item.title: Britney Spears Pleads Guilty to Reckless Driving After D.U.I. Charge
item.description: As part of a plea deal, the pop star will serve 12 months probation, complete a three-month substance abuse program and pay a modest fine.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens and Esmé E. Deprez
item.media:credit: Danny Moloshok/Reuters
item.media:description: A representative for Britney Spears said after her arrest that her actions were “completely inexcusable.”

item.title: This Professor Won the ‘Bauhaus Bathroom’ Design Competition for the Gropius House
item.description: Announcing the winner of a competition to create a public restroom at the historic Gropius House.
item.dc:creator: Jane Margolies

item.title: They Went Viral Online as Dancing Prodigies. Here’s What They’re Up to Now.
item.description: Performers who wowed us online as children are coming of age. But they have few obvious career paths.
item.dc:creator: Margaret Fuhrer
item.media:credit: Scott Rossi for The New York Times

item.title: Ken Griffin Acquires Second Original Printing of the Constitution
item.description: The document, purchased in a private sale for an undisclosed amount, will be on view in New York for the 250th anniversary of American independence.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler

item.title: A ‘Lord of the Flies’ for the ‘Adolescence’ Era
item.description: A new TV series based on the William Golding classic, written by Jack Thorne, explores how boys are susceptible to the influence of others.
item.dc:creator: Imogen West-Knights
item.media:credit: J Redza/Eleven/Sony Pictures Television
item.media:description: David McKenna as Piggy, left, and Winston Sawyers as Ralph, center, in Jack Thorne’s “Lord of The Flies.”

item.title: Chasing Stardom in Korea Nearly Destroyed Ejae. Then Came ‘KPop Demon Hunters.’
item.description: Ejae, the movie’s breakout star, was the face of K-pop in 2025. Facing questions of art, national belonging and authenticity, she wants to be much more.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim
item.media:credit: Woohae Cho for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ejae in the Hannam neighborhood of Seoul in April. She spent about a decade training to be a K-pop star in the city.

item.title: The Met Makes a Statement With 9 New Mannequin Bodies
item.description: The latest Costume Institute exhibition expands its ideas of who, exactly, belongs in fashion. Will the gala follow suit?
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Paul Westlake, via The Metropolitan Museum of Art
item.media:description: Five of the new mannequins in the new Costume Institute exhibition, each of which have been modeled on actual people.

item.title: Streisand, LuPone, Holliday, Menzel: Broadway’s Biggest Voices
item.description: Lea Michele, Christine Ebersole, Jennifer Hudson, Kristin Chenoweth, Adam Lambert and 10 others reflect on their favorite belters.

item.title: The Zen of Peter Frampton
item.description: Grappling with a progressive muscular disease and making a new album with his son, the onetime British guitar hero may be happier than ever.
item.dc:creator: Grayson Haver Currin and Eric Ryan Anderson
item.media:credit: Eric Ryan Anderson for The New York Times

item.title: Surreal, Cryptic and Just Plain Strange: 4 Fantastical Places in Italy
item.description: Artists, an architect and a secretive commune built these otherworldly marvels — and you can visit them.
item.dc:creator: Laura Rysman
item.media:credit: Pontus Berghe
item.media:description: In Tuscany, at a sculpture park called the Tarot Garden, which was created by the artist Niki de Saint Phalle, a hulking monster-like sculpture marks the entrance to the park with a waterfall spilling from its mouth.

item.title: ‘Lord of the Flies,’ and 7 More Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: A new adaptation of the 1954 novel comes to Netflix, and “Outlander” wraps up its eight-season run.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: via Netflix
item.media:description: This four-episode iteration of “The Lord of the Flies” was first released in Britain on BBC.
